description: If 'A implies B' must be true, and A must be true, then B follows — even across worlds.

guides.text: This is the **K axiom** of modal logic: if $A \to B$ is necessarily true and $A$ is necessarily true, then $B$ is necessarily true. All in propositional logic mode — the □ operator is our new primitive. We'll prove it as an implication.
Two nested implications. Assume the outermost: $\Box (A \to B)$.
Now prove the inner implication $\Box A \to \Box B$. Assume the antecedent: $\Box A$.
We have $\Box (A \to B)$ at step 1 and $\Box A$ at step 2. The rule `box-elim` is modus ponens under the box — from $\Box (A \to B)$ and $\Box A$, derive $\Box B$.
Discharge the inner assumption $\Box A$ to form $\Box A \to \Box B$.
Discharge the outer assumption $\Box (A \to B)$ to complete the K axiom.
